When following the Hebrew text, יבב corresponds to making a תרועה blast as opposed to tqˁ = תקע, which appears to be the more general term for any kind of sounding of the horn.
In the biblical contexts narrating the sounds of war, it is difficult to know whether the Aramaic translators/scribes understood thus word to refer to a trumpet or an oral sound.
Note that the Dt forms explicitly render a biblical hithpa'el.

The Tetragram is indicated variously in Jewish manuscripts by various replacements including יוי, ייי, ה׳, and two yodhs with or without a supralinear yodh between them, originally perhaps reflecting just a series of small strokes. In no case though was it to be read as written. Note that this form is mostly left unvocalized in Onqelos/Jonathan traditions but (partially?) vocalized with the vowels of Adonai some of those mss. and in the Genizah ms.

entries.form_section: In Syr: the abs. is yōm/yūm and the most common pl. is the emphatic formyawmāṯā while the absolute and construct have the masculine plural ending as does the emphatic following numbers; BA yowmAtA) ; elsewhere ya/ōm, yaw/ōmīn; there appears to be a lexical distinction between the two plurals in the older dialects, with yawmīn referring to literal "days," while yawmāṯā refers rather to "time, times," but they can be used interchangeably even in the same text: see Hugoye 14(2011):55.
entries.end_notes:
The Mandaic expression "riding on the day" undoubtedly reflects the common Akkadian expression RKB ūmē said of the gods "riding on a storm wind" (see Müller-Kessler, "Aramäische Beschwörungen und astronomische Omina ...", pp. 433-35), but the word iuma was surely always simply understood as "day" within Mandaic.
As for the expression using the repeated plural in the KTMW insription, we believe that the commonly agreed upon interpretation of similar expressions in the MT as "yearly" is unwarranted and should be rendered "from time to time" and would so suggest is the best understanding of the KTMW passage as well.

entries.primary_gloss: D to set down, fix firmly
entries.form_section: D with an unusually long thematic vowel, perhaps to distinguish it semantically from yḥṭ #1?
entries.end_notes:
Modern lexicographers tend to understand the Biblical Aramaic usage as "to repair" or "to examine" (cf. HALOT:"while we cannot work out the precise meaning the most plausible translation can be taken as to repair"), and take the root to be חוט).
To judge by the Qumran usage, however, the BA was by then understood to have the meaning 'set down, put in firm position" and the quite independent ADAB exxs. confirm a similar usage and this spelling. Thus, this lemma is very probably to be joined with yḥṭ#1, as it is connected with the fundamental idea of "to drop down".
NB: Some authors continue to argue that the root is middle weak and the forms with initial yodh are imperfects used in past contexts. This is impossible, as the (non-jussive) imperfect plural in Aramaic ends in nūn.

entries.form_section: Zenjirli (Sam'al) wt; JBAmb also ywt ; JLA always with long vowel, while PTA Genizah mss. distinguish between yaṯ in construct with a following noun and yāṯ with pronominal suffixes: yAthON , etc.; see the similar pattern with lwt prep.
entries.end_notes:
In Eastern Aramaic only in archaic and dialectal contexts, when quoting scripture, or with certain verbs, such as šyn "to pacify", where the semantic context borders on that of the derived nominal yt. Even in reference to Genesis some of the Syriac commentators took the usage to hint at the nominal form (for which see BHCandBak [75]1f.)
In some LJLA texts, it is also found with the subject of some verbs (our mng. 4) in a manner somewhat different from the similar occasional BH usage of את, the derived nominal, or the pronominal usage. We take this practice to be ungrammatical due to scribal/authorial uncertainty as to the rection of the verbs in question.
One needs also to recognize the grammaticalization of this form as an infix before pronominal suffixes on verbs, especially in later western Aramaic dialects and (perhaps erroneously?) in JBA texts.

entries.form_section: BA, JLA impf. yit.ib ; Syr )iteb , net.eb ; JBA )ytyb , ytyb; Mand. ˁtib/nitib imv tib, ˁtib (sg. and pl.), ˁtibiun (pl.) ENA (y)tāwā.
entries.end_notes:
The G p.p. is generally used in Syriac for the non-progressive present tense instead of the active ptcp. (but see also s.v. ytyb adj.) In unvocalized Jewish texts, however, forms such as יתיב should probably be taken as the active ptcp. whatever the sense, given that explicit p.p. forms are not found in the best vocalized texts. On the other hand, in LJLA texts the bare יתיב is most often just the simple 3.m.s. perfect.
The finite forms regularly govern preps b- and ˁl, but both active and passive G participles are often used simply in construct: u-ma-ia-a-ti-ib-a-a-)-i and those who dwell in it Uruk 13.
In targumic texts, this verb can match the BH cognate when the sense is nomadic settlement (as at Gen. 20:1), though that is probably no longer a true Aramaic usage.
